does: This paper introduces a three-layer architecture for privacy-preserving language model personalization that physically separates user-specific information from shared model weights. The system combines a static base model, composable domain-expert LoRA adapters that modify behavior without embedding user data, and per-user proxy artifacts that can be deleted to achieve deterministic unlearning. The authors evaluate their approach on Phi-3.5-mini and Llama-3.1-8B, demonstrating that removing a user's proxy artifact returns model outputs to baseline behavior (KL divergence ~0.21 nats, 82-89% verification success rate) with negligible cross-user contamination. By architecture, user-specific information never enters shared weights, eliminating model inversion, membership inference, and training-data extraction attacks against the shared components.
connects: This work addresses fundamental limitations in prior personalization approaches that embed user information directly into model weights, making machine unlearning computationally intractable and requiring full retraining. It builds on recent advances in parameter-efficient fine-tuning through LoRA adapters, which enable lightweight model specialization, while incorporating insights from deterministic unlearning literature that seeks efficient data deletion. The paper combines modular adaptation techniques with the emerging line of work on privacy-preserving personalization, extending prior efforts that struggled with isolating user data and proving its removal. The architecture responds to growing regulatory pressure for user data deletion rights and practical concerns about privacy vulnerabilities in personalized models.
enables: This architecture opens pathways toward practical machine unlearning at scale by converting deletion from an intractable optimization problem into a deterministic operation, potentially enabling real-time user data removal compliance. The separable design suggests applications in federated learning scenarios where privacy-preserving personalization is essential, and in multi-tenant systems requiring strong isolation guarantees. Future work could explore combining this approach with differential privacy techniques to provide formal privacy guarantees, extending the architecture to larger models and more complex personalization patterns, or developing more sophisticated adapter compositions. The framework may inspire new research into architectural designs that make privacy properties provable by construction rather than empirically verified, and could inform standards for privacy-respecting commercial LLM deployment.
